html:
CAAS: the market is pricing the wrong movie

China Automotive Systems is not a collapsing $300 million revenue steering supplier. The latest public numbers show the opposite: 2025 revenue hit a record $765.7 million, EPS was $1.42, operating income was $53.6 million, and operating cash flow was $111.6 million. Management guided 2026 revenue to $810 million, and the company has moved to semiannual reporting, so there is no reported Q1 2026 collapse to underwrite.

At $4.73, CAAS is a $142.7 million market cap company with 30.17 million shares. Year-end cash, pledged cash, short-term investments, and long-term deposits were $256.7 million, debt was about $87 million, and net cash was $169.7 million. After the post-year-end $15.8 million Mexico property purchase, pro forma net cash is still roughly $154 million, or about $5.10 per share. The stock is trading below pro forma net cash while the operating business is earning real money.

The variant view is that CAAS is not just a cheap China auto supplier. It is a cheap, controlled, PFIC-tainted China auto supplier that is trying to become a global intelligent steering supplier while nobody with institutional size can easily own it. EPS products grew 25.5% in 2025 and reached 41.5% of revenue. Gross margin rose to 19.0% from 16.8%. Q4’s 23.1% gross margin included one-time tariff refunds and depreciation benefits, so it should not be annualized, but management still said 2026 gross margin should remain at a healthy level.

The operating transition matters. CAAS is moving from hydraulic steering into electric power steering, R-EPS, active rear-wheel steering, high-torque steering motors, and intelligent chassis components. That is the boring physical layer underneath ADAS and EV platform complexity. The company has launched rear-wheel active steering aimed at RMB 200,000 EVs, brought high-torque intelligent steering motors toward mass production, and disclosed a European R-EPS order with expected annual sales above $100 million beginning around 2027. In December it also won its first large-scale South American C-EPS contract for a vehicle platform with planned annual sales above 300,000 units, with production expected in early 2028.

The Mexico angle is real but should be framed correctly. CAAS already had $121.6 million of North American sales in 2025, up 15.3%, serving customers including Stellantis and Ford. Its 20-F says Chinese-made steering gears exported to the U.S. may face stacked tariffs around 62.5%, and management said on the call that current tariffs remain near 60%. The March 2026 purchase of property in Derramadero, Saltillo, Coahuila is therefore not random real estate. It is a probable attempt to localize production near the North American auto corridor and protect or expand an existing customer base. The risk is that U.S. policy increasingly targets Chinese-linked auto supply chains, including connected vehicle software and hardware rules, so Mexico is not a clean loophole. But even partial localization can be valuable if it converts tariff-burdened North American revenue into USMCA-adjacent local production.

The broader industry backdrop is not perfect, but it favors CAAS’s mix shift. China’s 2025 auto market hit record volume, and 2026 is slower, but NEVs are still taking share. In April, NEVs were 53.2% of new car sales, and first-four-month auto exports were up 61.5%, according to CAAM data reported by China.org.cn. CAAS does not need a heroic China unit cycle. It needs higher steering content per vehicle, export platform wins, and enough global growth to prove that 2025 was not a one-off.

Why does this exist? PFIC classification, no expected QEF information, China governance risk, Han family control, tiny liquidity, and near-zero institutional sponsorship. The 20-F shows management owning about 64.8% and public float around 35.2%. Retail/social context is thin: scattered Reddit value posts, automated filing summaries, and low Stocktwits-style chatter. Short interest is tiny, so this is not a squeeze. It is a neglect trade.

The central risk is not accounting optics. It is whether the cash is real, accessible, and shareholder-relevant. A company can trade below net cash for years if minority investors never see the cash. CAAS paid a special dividend in 2024, and the CFO said he would recommend restarting buybacks after the Cayman redomiciliation, but investors need proof. Semiannual reporting also makes the story harder to follow. If H1 2026 shows margin fade, working-capital absorption, or Mexico spending without contract detail, the discount can persist.

Still, the asymmetry is unusual. The market is valuing the operating business at less than zero despite 2026 revenue guidance of $810 million and a credible path to $1.15 to $1.50 of forward EPS even after normalizing Q4 benefits. A 3x to 4x forward P/E might be defensible for a controlled China microcap. Trading below net cash with an active global order book is harder to defend.

Bear case: $3.25, down 31%. China demand weakens, 2025 cash flow proves working-capital timing, margins fall toward 2024 levels, Mexico burns cash, and U.S. rules impair North American growth. The stock trades at roughly 0.25x book and investors heavily haircut cash.

Base case: $8.50, up 80%. 2026 revenue reaches management’s $810 million target, EPS normalizes around $1.25 to $1.40, Mexico shows a credible customer path, and the market gives CAAS 5x earnings plus partial credit for excess cash.

Bull case: $15.00, up 217%. European R-EPS, South America EPS, Malaysia, Brazil, and Mexico turn CAAS into a visible global steering supplier. EPS approaches $1.80 to $2.00 by 2027-2028, the company buys back stock or returns cash, and the multiple moves to 7x to 8x despite the China discount.

Current stock price: $4.73. The market is paying below pro forma net cash for an $800 million revenue business that is already profitable and moving into higher-content steering systems. That is the thesis.
